text.title: Release Strategy: Canary / Blue-Green / Feature-Flag Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Release Strategy: Canary / Blue-Green / Feature-Flag application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Pick by shape: stateless web → canary; stateful DB → blue-green with read-replica swap; client-facing experimental → feature flag; multi-region critical → ring deploy.
rule.rationale: One-size release strategy fails 50%+ of services; shape-matching is the design rule.

rule.statement: Each strategy has an observation window before next ring (canary: 5-15 min for stateless web; ring: 24h between rings). Skipping observation = uncontrolled rollout.
rule.rationale: Observation is the only thing turning canary from theater into safety; skipping it removes the value.

rule.statement: Rollback triggers: error rate > baseline + 50%, latency p99 > 2× baseline, business metric drop > 10%. Auto-rollback on breach.
rule.rationale: Manual rollback under stress is slow; pre-defined triggers + automation collapse decision time.

rule.statement: Feature flag = product strategy, not release strategy. Use both: deploy via canary, expose via flag. Conflating them loses both safety + agility.
rule.rationale: Industry experience: deploy-by-flag-only skips observation; observation-only with no flag delays product.

rule.statement: Feature flags + canary configs have cleanup policy: flags >90 days = audit-remove; canary configs >30 days = standard.
rule.rationale: Flag/config detritus is the silent infra debt source; cleanup policy is the only mitigation.
